title: Resin Braziers
desc: Every section of the lounge wants a different scent profile, and every regular swears they can tell when it is wrong.

steps.text:
The lounge does not smell like the surrounding desert, and that is very much the point. Its braziers burn little cones of resin, spice, and dried flower dust chosen for the room they serve: cooler notes for the card alcoves, richer smoke for the high tables, a cleaner blend near the grooming chairs where too much fragrance would interfere with oils and polish. Get the mixes wrong and somebody notices. Get them badly wrong and somebody with rank notices loudly.

The scent mixer has three trays of prepared resins and no patience left. "Blend for the card alcoves if you want precise work," she says. "Or clear and reset the high-table braziers if you prefer heat, ash, and criticism from creatures who think smoke should obey class."

options.label: Blend the cooler resin cones for the card alcoves
Clear and reset the richer braziers at the high tables

steps.text:
The card alcoves need a scent light enough not to flatten conversation or distract from the quiet snap of tiles, counters, and polished fingernails against wood. You grind pale resin with mint dust and a breath of dried blossom, then press the mixture into neat cones that will burn slow and clean in the low bowls. It is careful work because too much flower makes the blend sentimental and too much mint turns the whole alcove medicinal.

When the first cone takes, the smoke rises cool and narrow, barely visible in the lamp light. The mixer leans in, judges the balance by habit, and nods. "Good. Sharp enough to wake the mind. Subtle enough not to announce it." In the Lounge, subtlety is often just precision with better manners.

options.label: Set the finished cones in the alcove tray and step back

steps.text:
The high-table braziers are wider, hotter, and far less forgiving. They burn richer resin so the smoke hangs just long enough to soften the air around the raised seating platforms and carry a sense of expense without suffocating anyone underneath it. You clear the old ash, scrape the clogged vents at the base, and build a fresh bed of charcoal before setting the dark resin cones into place.

The first burn comes up wrong, too thick and bitter, which tells you one of the vents is still fouled. You clear it, reset the cone, and try again. This time the smoke lifts in a slow warm ribbon, heavier than the card blend but still refined enough not to turn the high tables into a cookfire. The mixer smells the air, then looks at the rebalanced bowl with approval. "There. Wealth, not panic."

options.label: Stack the ash pan aside and leave the high tables properly scented
